title: AP 物理C力学 2013年国际卷

questions.prompt: 1. How far does the object travel during the first 10 s of its motion?
2. What is the instantaneous net force that acts on the object at $t=2 \mathrm{~s}$ ?
3. How much work is done by the net force between $t=0$ and $t=1 \mathrm{~s}$ ?

4. Persons $X, Y$, and $Z$ walk along a circular path of radius 50 m . Person $X$ walks halfway around the path, Person $Y$ walks $3 / 4$ of the way around the path, and Person $Z$ walks completely around the path. Which of the following correctly lists the walkers in order of the magnitudes of their displacement vectors from the least to the greatest?

5. A small particle starts from rest from the origin of an $x y$-coordinate system and travels in the $x y$-plane. Its acceleration in the $x$-direction is $2.0 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$, and its acceleration in the $y$-direction is $1.0 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$. What is the $x$-coordinate of the particle when its $y$-coordinate is 12 m ?
6. If the speed of an object moving in a straight line is increasing at a constant rate, the net force acting on the object is

7. A force $F$ accelerates a system of two blocks, $X$ and $Y$, on a horizontal frictionless surface, as shown above. The acceleration is $4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$. The force with which block $Y$ pushes on block $X$ has magnitude
8. A hoop of mass $m$ and radius $r$ rolls with constant speed on a horizontal surface without slipping. What is the hoop's translational kinetic energy divided by its rotational kinetic energy?
9. The rotational inertia of a sphere of mass $M$ and radius $R$ about a diameter is $\frac{2}{5} M R^{2}$. The rotational inertia about an axis tangent to the sphere is
10. All of the following are vector quantities EXCEPT

11. The toy raft shown in the top view above is sliding due east on frictionless ice at constant speed $v_{0}$ when a fan blows on its sail, producing a constant acceleration a directed to the north. Which of the following could indicate the path of the raft while the fan is blowing?

12. The velocity $v$ in meters per second of an object moving in a straight line is given as a function of time $t$ in seconds by $v=4 t^{3}+2 t$. The total distance the object travels between $t=1 \mathrm{~s}$ and $t=2 \mathrm{~s}$ is
13. Two small spheres, each with a mass of 3.0 kg , are placed 6.0 m apart. Which of the following is the order of magnitude of the gravitational attraction between them?

14. A satellite of mass $m$ is in a circular orbit with speed $v_{0}$ at a distance $r$ from the center of Earth. What is the ratio of its escape speed from the orbit to its orbital speed, $v_{e} / v_{0}$ ?

15. The block shown above is attached to a rigid wall by means of an ideal spring and moves on a horizontal surface with negligible friction. The block oscillates with a period $T$. If a second ideal spring, identical to the first, is placed above the first spring and attached to the wall and the block, the new oscillation period of the block is

16. An object on the end of a spring of force constant $k$ moves in simple harmonic motion with amplitude $A$ and frequency $f$. Which of the following is a possible expression for the potential energy of the spring as a function of time $t$ ?

17. A wheel with rotational inertia $0.04 \mathrm{~kg} \cdot \mathrm{~m}^{2}$ and radius 0.02 m is turning at the rate of 10 revolutions per second when a frictional torque is applied to stop it. How much work is done by the torque in stopping the wheel?

18. A uniform beam of weight $W$ is attached to a wall by a pivot at one end and is held horizontal by a cable attached to the other end of the beam and to the wall, as shown above. $T$ is the tension in the cable, which makes an angle $\theta$ with the beam. Which of the following is equal to $T$ ?

19. An arrow of mass $m$ and speed $v_{0}$ strikes and sticks to one end of a meterstick of mass $M$ as shown in the diagram above. The meterstick is initially at rest on a horizontal surface and free to move without friction. The speed of the center of mass of the stick-arrow system after the arrow strikes is given by which of the following expressions?

20. Object $X$ of mass $m$ is moving to the right with a speed of $3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ when it collides with object $Y$ of mass $m$ that is moving to the right with a speed of $2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, as shown above. After the collision, $X$ is moving to the right with a speed of $2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and $Y$ is moving to the right with a speed of $3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. Which of the following is true of the collision?
21. What is the coefficient of friction between the box and the floor?
22. Which of the following best describes the frictional forces exerted on the box \section*{Box}

23. A block of mass $m$ is pulled across a rough horizontal surface at constant speed by a force of magnitude $F$, which acts at an angle of $\theta$ to the horizontal, as shown above. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the block and the surface is
24. The rope-and-pulley system of negligible mass shown above supports a block of weight $W$ that is at rest. If the tension throughout the rope is uniform, what is the reading on the spring scale?

25. The uniform rod of length $L$ shown above is supported by holding end $X$ so that the rod makes an angle $60^{\circ}$ with the horizontal floor. There is no friction acting between the rod and the floor. When the support at $X$ is removed, the rod falls under the influence of gravity. Which of the following best describes the movement of end $Y$ as the rod falls?

26. In an experiment, an object is released from rest from the top of a building. Its speed $v$ is measured as it reaches a point that is a distance $d$ from the point of release. If this distance was doubled, what would the new speed be, assuming air resistance is negligible?

27. A particle moves on a straight line with a speed $v$ that changes with time $t$ according to the equation $v=v_{0} e^{-\beta t}$, where $v_{0}$ and $\beta$ are constants. The acceleration $a$ of the particle is related to its speed by which of the following equations?

28. The relationship between the magnitude of the restoring force $F$ and the resultant displacement $x$ from equilibrium for a nonlinear spring is given by the equation $F=k x^{2}$. What is the potential energy of the spring when it has been compressed a distance $x_{0}$ ?

29. The period $T$ of a pendulum of length $L$ is measured to determine $g$ at the surface of Earth. The equation used is $T=2 \pi \sqrt{L / g}$. The mass of the pendulum bob is 10.0 kg , and the length of the pendulum is 1.00 m . Which of the following would contribute most to the expected error in the result?
30. If a person goes to the bottom of a deep mine shaft on a planet of uniform density, which of the following is true?

31. A certain satellite in a circular orbit of radius $r$ remains vertically above a certain point on Earth's equator. Which of the following expressions correctly gives $r$ in terms of $\omega$ (the angular velocity of Earth's rotation about its axis), $M$ (the mass of Earth), and $G$ (the universal gravitational constant) ?

32. Two blocks of masses $M$ and $2 M$ are on a frictionless horizontal surface, as shown above, and are held in place with a compressed spring of negligible mass between them. If the blocks are then released and the block of mass $2 M$ leaves the spring with a velocity $v$, the velocity of the center of mass of the blocks is
33. What is the magnitude of the average force exerted by the racket on the ball?
34. How does the magnitude of the average force exerted by the ball on the racket compare to the magnitude of the average force exerted by the racket on the ball?

35. A circular platform has a radius $R$ and rotational inertia $I$. The platform rotates around a fixed pivot at its center with negligible friction and an initial angular velocity $\omega$. A child of mass $m$ (represented by the small circle in the figure above) runs tangentially with speed $v$ and jumps on the outer edge of the platform. When the child is standing on the outer edge of the platform, the new angular velocity is

A ball of mass $m$ and cross-sectional area $A$ is released from rest near the surface of Earth. The ball experiences a resistive force due to the air that is proportional to the ball's velocity, $\mathbf{F}_{r}=-b A \mathbf{v}$, where $b$ is a positive constant. Determine all algebraic answers in terms of $m, b, A$, and fundamental constants.

parts.partContent: Draw free-body diagrams for the ball for the following situations. Give each vector a descriptive label and draw them approximately to scale.
Calculate the terminal velocity $v_{T}$ of the ball.
Starting from Newton's laws, derive an expression for the time required for the ball to reach one third the terminal velocity.
Sketch the following two graphs of the ball's motion from when the ball is released at time $t=0$ until it approaches terminal velocity.

questions.prompt: A 0.30 kg cart moving at $2.0 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ to the right collides with a 0.10 kg cart, initially moving at $3.0 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ to the left. After the collision, the 0.10 kg cart moves to the right with a speed of $1.2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. Assume friction is negligible.

parts.partContent: Calculate the magnitude and direction of the velocity of the 0.30 kg cart after the collision.

Is this an elastic collision? Justify your answer.

The 0.30 kg cart is now removed. The 0.10 kg cart continues to move to the right and then collides with and compresses an ideal spring whose right end is attached to a fixed wall.
Calculate the potential energy of the spring when it reaches maximum compression.
The maximum compression of the spring is 0.10 m . Calculate the spring constant of the spring.

Suppose instead that the spring is nonlinear such that the force $F$ due to the spring when it is compressed a distance $x$ is $F=-b x^{3}$. Calculate the value of the constant $b$ if the spring is compressed 0.10 m .

questions.prompt: A solid disk of radius 0.10 m is mounted on a vertical axis. A string of negligible mass is wrapped around the rim of the disk; passes over a small, lightweight, frictionless pulley as shown above; and is tied to a block of mass 0.05 kg . The system is released from rest, and a computer records the velocity of the falling block as a function of time, as shown below.

parts.partContent: Using the graph above, calculate the acceleration of the falling block.
Use your result from part
to calculate the rotational inertia of the disk.
Calculate the angular momentum of the disk at time $t=0.45 \mathrm{~s}$.

The disk is removed and replaced with a hoop of the same mass and radius, but with all of its mass concentrated near the rim of the hoop and connected to the axis by lightweight spokes. The experiment is then repeated. Is the angular acceleration of the hoop greater than, less than, or the same as that of the solid disk?

Greater than
Less than
The same
Justify your answer.

STOP


THE FOLLOWING INSTRUCTIONS APPLY TO THE COVERS OF THE SECTION II BOOKLET.
- MAKE SURE YOU HAVE COMPLETED THE IDENTIFICATION INFORMATION AS REQUESTED ON THE FRONT AND BACK COVERS OF THE SECTION II BOOKLET.
- CHECK TO SEE THAT YOUR AP NUMBER LABEL APPEARS IN THE BOX(ES) ON THE COVER(S).
- MAKE SURE YOU HAVE USED THE SAME SET OF AP NUMBER LABELS ON ALL AP EXAMS YOU HAVE TAKEN THIS YEAR.

4 points


For having all four forces properly labeled and in the proper direction
For having both $W$ vectors of equal length
For having $F_{r}<W$ a short time after the ball is released
For having $F_{r}=W$ at terminal velocity
Note: 1 earned point was deducted if there were any extraneous forces

2 points

For setting the resistive force equal to the weight when at terminal velocity
$$
F_{r}=W
$$

Substituting correct expressions for both forces
$$
b A v_{T}=m g
$$

For the correct magnitude of the terminal velocity
$$
v_{T}=\frac{m g}{b A}
$$

Distribution of points

\title{
AP ${ }^{\text {® }}$ PHYSICS C: MECHANICS \\ 2013 SCORING GUIDELINES
}

4 points

For a correct differential equation expressing Newton's 2nd law
$F=m a=m g-b A v$
$m \frac{d v}{d t}=m g-b A v$
Solving the differential equation for $v$
$\frac{m}{b A} \frac{d v}{d t}=\frac{m g}{b A}-v$ where $v_{T}=\frac{m g}{b A}$
$\frac{m}{b A} \cdot \frac{d v}{d t}=v_{T}-v$
$\frac{d v}{v_{T}-v}=\frac{b A}{m} d t$
$\int_{0}^{v} \frac{d v}{v_{T}-v}=\int_{0}^{t} \frac{b A}{m} d t$
This is of the form $\int \frac{d u}{u}=\ln |u|$, where $u=v_{T}-v$ and $d u=-d v$.
$\left[-\ln \left|v_{T}-v\right|\right]_{0}^{v}=\left[\frac{b A}{m} t\right]_{0}^{t}$
$\ln \left(v_{T}-v\right)-\ln \left(v_{T}\right)=-\frac{b A}{m} t$
$\ln \left(\frac{v_{T}-v}{v_{T}}\right)=-\frac{b A}{m} t$
$\frac{v_{T}-v}{v_{T}}=e^{-b A t / m}$
$v(t)=v_{T}\left(1-e^{-b A t / m}\right)$
For a correct expression of velocity as a function of time
$$
v(t)=\frac{m g}{b A}\left(1-e^{-b A t / m}\right)
$$

For substituting $v_{T} / 3$ into the above equation
$v_{T} / 3=v_{T}\left(1-e^{-b A t / m}\right)$
Solving the above equation for time
$1 / 3=1-e^{-b A t / m}$
$e^{-b A t / m}=1-1 / 3=2 / 3$
$-b A t / m=\ln (2 / 3)$

\title{
AP ${ }^{\text {® }}$ PHYSICS C: MECHANICS \\ 2013 SCORING GUIDELINES
}

5 points


For an acceleration graph that does NOT begin at the origin

For an acceleration graph that has a horizontal asymptote of $a=0$

For a velocity graph that begins at the origin

For a velocity graph that has a horizontal asymptote of $v=v_{T}$ or $-v_{T}$, as appropriate

For having both graphs with proper and accurate curvature

4 points

Distribution of points

For a correct statement of the conservation of momentum
1 point
$p_{i}=p_{f}$
$m_{1} v_{1 i}+m_{2} v_{2 i}=m_{1} v_{1 f}+m_{2} v_{2 f}$
For correctly substituting into the above equation
$(0.30 \mathrm{~kg})(2.0 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s})+(0.10 \mathrm{~kg})(-3.0 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s})=(0.30 \mathrm{~kg}) v_{1 f}+(0.10 \mathrm{~kg})(1.2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s})$
For a correct answer with units
$v_{1 f}=0.60 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
For correctly indicating the direction is to the right

2 points

For answering "no", or that the collision is inelastic

For a correct justification

Examples:
No, the collision is not elastic. As shown in the calculations below, the final kinetic energy is less than the initial kinetic energy.
$$

$$

No, the collision is not elastic. Since $v_{1 f}<v_{1 i}$ and $v_{2 f}<v_{2 i}, K_{f}$ must be less than $K_{i}$.
No, the collision is not elastic. The final relative velocity is less than the initial relative velocity.

3 points

For a correct statement of the conservation of energy
$K_{1}=U_{S 2}$ or $\frac{1}{2} m_{1} v_{1}^{2}=U_{S 2}$
For correctly substituting into the above equation
$U_{S 2}=\frac{1}{2}(0.10 \mathrm{~kg})(1.2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s})^{2}$
For a correct answer with units
$U_{S 2}=0.072 \mathrm{~J}$
Note: If the student uses the wrong cart, mixes up values for mass and/or velocity, or has a sign error, only the first point can be earned.

3 points

For a correct expression for the potential energy of a spring
$U=\frac{1}{2} k x^{2}$
Substituting into the above equation
$(0.072 \mathrm{~J})=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)(k)(0.10 \mathrm{~m})^{2}$
For an answer consistent with part

1 point
For correct units
1 point
$k=14.4 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}\left(\right.$ or $\left.\mathrm{kg} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right)$
Note: There was no penalty for a sign error. If the wrong cart was used in part

3 points

For setting up a correct integral
$U=-\int F d x=\int b x^{3} d x$
For a correct equation for the potential energy of the spring
$U=\frac{1}{4} b x^{4}$
Substituting into the above equation
$(0.072)=\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)

and with correct sign and units
$b=2880 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{3}\left[\right.$ or $\left.\mathrm{kg} /\left(\mathrm{m}^{2} \cdot \mathrm{~s}^{2}\right)\right]$
Note: If the wrong cart was used in part

4 points

Distribution of points

For correctly expressing the acceleration as the slope or rate of change of velocity
$a=$ slope $=\frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t}$
For using two points from a best-fit line to calculate the slope/acceleration (not data points unless the data points are on the line)
$a=\frac{(1.52-0.71) \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}}{(0.55-0.25) \mathrm{s}}$
For a reasonable numerical value for $a$

For correct units (was earned even if numerical answer was not correct)
$a=2.7 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2} \quad$ (acceptable range of answers: 2.5 to 3.3 )

5 points

For a correct expression of Newton's 2nd law for rotational motion
$\tau=I \alpha$
$T R=I \alpha$
For a correct substitution for the angular acceleration in terms of the linear acceleration
$\alpha=a / R$
$T R=I(a / R)$
$I=\frac{T R^{2}}{a}$
For a correct expression of Newton's 2nd law for translational motion
$F_{n e t}=m a$
$m g-T=m a$
$T=m(g-a)$
Substituting the expression for $T$ into the expression for the rotational inertia
$I=\frac{T R^{2}}{a}=\frac{m(g-a) R^{2}}{a}$
Substituting values into the above equation
$I=\frac{(0.05 \mathrm{~kg})\left(9.8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}-2.7 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right)(0.10 \mathrm{~m})^{2}}{\left(2.7 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right)}$
For a numerical answer consistent with part

For correct units
$I=0.0013 \mathrm{~kg} \cdot \mathrm{~m}^{2}$ (the acceptable range for $a$ gives a range of 0.000985 to 0.00146 , or using $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$ a range of 0.00101 to 0.0015

4 points

For a correct expression for angular momentum
$L=I \omega$
For correct substitution into any proper rotational kinematics expression
$\omega=\alpha t$ and $\alpha=a / R \quad$ or $\quad \omega=v / R$ and $v=a t$
$L=I a t / R$
Substituting into the above equation
$L=\left(0.0013 \mathrm{~kg} \cdot \mathrm{~m}^{2}\right)\left(2.7 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right)(0.45 \mathrm{~s}) /(0.10 \mathrm{~m})$
For a numerical answer consistent with part

For correct units
$L=0.016 \mathrm{~kg} \cdot \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$
An alternate solution using $L=I v / R$ and using the graph to determine $v$ at $t=0.45 \mathrm{~s}$ was also acceptable.

2 points

For correctly selecting "Less than"

For a correct justification

Examples:
The same force is applied to the system (or the same torque acts on the hoop/disk). Rotational inertia is higher for a hoop than a disk, so the acceleration will decrease.
Since the potential energy stays the same and the rotational inertia of a hoop is greater than that of a disk, more of the energy goes into rotating the disk and less into moving the block. So the linear acceleration of the block will be less with the hoop than with a disk. Since $\alpha=a / R$, the angular acceleration of the hoop will be less.
A derivation of $a=\frac{m g R^{2}}{I+m R^{2}}, \alpha=\frac{m g R}{I+m R^{2}}$, or an equivalent expression, and an explanation that the rotational inertia of the hoop is larger.
